title: UC1 — Mission control's roster won't come online
description: Today's mission for the Artemis pad shift: bring mission-control's on-shift roster online, then hand the diagnosis to artemis-mission-control-debugger through the kagent dashboard chat — UC1 is the participant's first contact with a kagent agent.

steps.title: Mission setup — bring today's roster online
steps.explanation:
Mission control is bringing today's on-shift roster online for the Artemis pad shift. Apply the manifests below to deploy `mission-control` to your vCluster — namespace, service, deployment. Once the apply completes, the roster should be reachable on the cluster.

The three files are dropped into your workspace by this step (`fileEdits`), then applied with a single `kubectl apply -f`.

fileEdits.content:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: artemis-uc1
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc1
    kagent-workshop/scenario: imagepullbackoff

apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: mission-control
  namespace: artemis-uc1
  labels:
    app: mission-control
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc1
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: mission-control
  ports:
    - name: http
      port: 8000
      targetPort: http
      protocol: TCP

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: mission-control
  namespace: artemis-uc1
  labels:
    app: mission-control
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc1
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: mission-control
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: mission-control
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: mission-control
          # Tag deliberately not published — produces ImagePullBackOff on first pull (FR-008).
          image: ghcr.io/kagent-workshop/mission-control:v999
          ports:
            - name: http
              containerPort: 8000
          env:
            - name: APP_IDENTITY
              value: mission-control
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "10m"
              memory: "64Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "100m"
              memory: "128Mi"

steps.title: Mission status check — is the roster live?
steps.explanation: Mission control should be online by now. Check that the roster pod has come up. Give the kubelet ~30 s to settle before running the command — if the pod is not Running, you'll see it here, and that's our cue to call in help.

steps.title: Call the agent for help
steps.explanation:
The roster is stuck and the pad shift starts soon. Hand the diagnosis to **`artemis-mission-control-debugger`** through the kagent dashboard.

The command below opens the dashboard (it port-forwards `kagent-ui` and points your browser at it). The command stays running in the terminal — leave it running while you use the chat, then `Ctrl+C` once you have the answer.

In the dashboard, open the chat for **`artemis-mission-control-debugger`** (namespace `artemis-uc1`) and paste the following prompt:

> The mission-control pod in the artemis-uc1 namespace is not coming up. Diagnose it.

The agent has the same Kubernetes read tools you ran by hand (`get pod`, `describe pod`, `get events`) — only it correlates them in one synthesis. Read the response directly in the chat surface.

*Beat 3 invocation form (frozen by STORY-031 spike): `kagent dashboard` foregrounds the port-forward and auto-opens the browser; alive until `Ctrl+C`.*

steps.title: What we'd have done by hand
steps.explanation:
Without the agent, you would have run three `kubectl` commands and joined the evidence yourself:

```bash
kubectl get pods -n artemis-uc1
kubectl describe pod -n artemis-uc1 -l app=mission-control
kubectl get events -n artemis-uc1 --sort-by=.lastTimestamp
```

Three pieces of state to combine into a verdict: the pod's `Waiting` phase, the container's `ImagePullBackOff` reason, and the kubelet's repeated pull-failed events. The agent ran the same three tools and returned a single sentence of root cause: **the deploy targets `mission-control:v999`, which was never published**.

You ran one command (`kubectl get pods`) just to see something was off — you didn't have to walk the rest. Multiplied across the dozen incidents an on-call rotation handles in a week, that's the value the workshop is selling. UC2 will scale this up to multi-resource correlation; UC3 to external observability; UC4 to multi-agent fan-out.
